links.rationale: Both require the student to point to specific text details/passages to support an explanation or answer; GA does not name inference, and because GA has no RL/RI split this same evidence-citing element also serves RI.4.1.
Both require quoting/referring to specific passages from a text to support an idea or answer; GA adds page-number recording and omits the inference demand.
Both ask the student to determine a theme of a literary text from details in the text; GA narrows the details to characters and does not include Ohio's summarizing sub-standard.
Both ask the student to determine a theme and explain how it is conveyed through how a character responds to challenges/conflict; GA lacks only Ohio's summary sub-standard.

Both ask the student to describe a character in depth from text details and how that character functions in the story; GA restricts the analysis to protagonist/antagonist and adds conflict, and omits setting and event.

Cross-grade (GA 3): GA's 'describe characters' traits, motivations, actions, thoughts, and feelings' is the same skill as Ohio's parenthetical (a character's thoughts, words, or actions), though GA additionally ties it to the central message.
Both require interpreting figurative language (similes, metaphors, idioms) as used in a text; GA asks for the impact of the figurative language rather than Ohio's determination of word meaning.

Both ask the student to explain the structural elements that define poetry as a form; GA covers only the poetry half of Ohio's poem/drama/prose comparison and says nothing about drama's structural elements.

Both ask the student to compare structural/organizational features across texts written in different genres (GA explicitly names rhyme pattern); GA constrains this to texts on the same topic and does not target genre-defining elements like verse, meter, or stage directions.

Both ask the student to explain how the parts of a text (chapters/sections) work together to create the overall structure; GA frames it as features and structure functioning in conjunction rather than Ohio's series-of-parts-to-whole.
Both ask the student to explain/compare the differing points of view and perspectives of narrators or speakers within a text.
Both ask the student to describe how a narrator's or speaker's perspective influences the text and how its events are presented.

Both ask the student to explain how non-prose/multimodal choices contribute to a text's overall effect; GA's demand is classifying texts by mode and its effect on audience/purpose, which is narrower than Ohio's analysis of visual elements' contribution to meaning, tone, and mood.
Near-verbatim: both ask the student to compare and contrast the treatment of similar themes and topics and patterns of events in stories, myths, and traditional literature from different cultures.
Near-verbatim: both ask the student to compare and contrast stories in the same genre on their approaches to similar themes and topics.

Both require the student to point to specific text passages to support an explanation or answer; GA does not name inference, and this element is shared with RL.4.1 because GA has no literature/informational strand split.
Both require quoting specific passages from a text to support an idea or answer; GA adds page-number recording and omits the inference demand.

Both center on the main idea of an expository text and the facts and key details that support it; GA's object is the techniques used to present the main idea rather than Ohio's determining it, and GA has no summarizing demand.

Both address multiple main ideas of an expository text supported by facts and key details; GA's demand is evaluating the presentation techniques rather than Ohio's determining the main ideas and summarizing.
Verbatim match: explain events, procedures, ideas, or concepts in a historical, scientific, or technical text, including what happened and why, based on specific information in the text.

Verbatim match: explain the relationships or interactions between two or more individuals, events, ideas, or concepts in a historical, scientific, or technical text based on specific information in the text.

Cross-grade (GA 3): both ask the student to describe how a text is organized to present information using organizational structures such as cause/effect and to refer to parts of the text; GA grade 4 replaces this describe-one-text skill with a compare-across-texts one, so the nearest same-skill element sits one grade below.

Cross-grade (GA 5): both ask the student to describe the overall organizational structure (GA names problem/solution) by which a text represents ideas and information; GA couples structure with text features.
Cross-grade (GA 4): both ask the student to compare and contrast the organizational structure of two or more texts; GA constrains the texts to the same topic in different genres.
Cross-grade (GA 5): both ask the student to compare and contrast firsthand and secondhand accounts of the same event or topic; GA has no grade-4 element for this skill.

Both ask the student to compare multiple accounts of the same event or topic and note how they differ; GA fixes the accounts as firsthand/secondhand rather than Ohio's open 'multiple accounts' and does not name perspective.

Cross-grade (GA 6): both ask the student to compare and contrast two authors' accounts/presentations of the same event or topic; GA restricts to historical events and does not name the perspectives represented.

Cross-grade (GA 3): both ask the student to identify non-prose features of visual/auditory modes and explain how they contribute to the text's purpose and effect; GA does not reach Ohio's interpretation of quantitative displays (charts, graphs, timelines), and GA has no closer element at grade 4 or 5.

Both ask the student to locate and draw information from multiple print and digital sources; GA frames this as conducting research on a self-selected topic rather than Ohio's efficient answer-finding/problem-solving.

Both ask the student to explain how an author uses evidence to support the points/claims made in a text; GA restricts the text type to opinion and argumentative texts, while Ohio allows any informational text.

Both ask the student to explain how an author's supporting reasons and evidence back the stated claim; GA restricts to opinion/argumentative texts and does not require Ohio's matching of specific evidence to corresponding points.
Both ask the student to integrate information from multiple texts on the same topic in relation to the important points and key details.

Cross-grade (GA 4): both ask the student to integrate information from several texts on the same topic; GA's grade-5 slot in this progression is occupied by firsthand/secondhand accounts, so the integrate-multiple-texts element sits one grade below.

unmatched.why: GA's only compare-and-contrast-characters/settings/events-within-a-text element is 3.T.T.1d, which is two grades below and therefore out of scope; GA grade 5's narrative element (5.T.T.1b) analyzes how setting/conflict/characterization contribute to plot, a different demand.

GA's Texts domain has no determine-word-meaning-in-context element, and nothing in GA addresses allusions to mythological characters; GA grade 4's craft element (4.T.SS.2a) is figurative language, which is Ohio's RL.5.4, not RL.4.4.

GA has no element asking students to compare a written story or drama with a visual or oral presentation of that same text; GA's mode elements ask students to classify texts by mode, not to compare versions of one text.
GA's 2023 standards contain no text-complexity-band reading standard and no text-to-self/text-to-text connection element; there is no GA counterpart to the CCSS anchor-10 shape.
GA's 2023 standards contain no text-complexity-band reading standard; the nearest read-and-comprehend elements (6.T.PM.1a/b) are period/genre knowledge standards, not complexity-band proficiency.

GA's Texts domain has no element for determining the meaning of general academic or domain-specific vocabulary in context; GA handles vocabulary outside the Texts domain, so no candidate covers this skill.
GA's Texts domain has no element for determining the meaning of general academic or domain-specific vocabulary in context.
GA's 2023 standards contain no text-complexity-band reading standard for informational/history/science/technical texts.
GA's 2023 standards contain no text-complexity-band reading standard for informational/history/science/technical texts.
